summary: Jeff Atwood examines a 2004 survey of self-installable blogging software that revealed a striking imbalance: while numerous PHP/MySQL solutions existed, .NET had only one or two options. He quotes Owen Winkler's criticism that .NET developers were so enamored with the language itself that they neglected user experience, installation simplicity, and practical functionality. Atwood acknowledges the criticism has merit, even after installing Community Server (the successor to .Text) himself. The post reflects early tensions between developer-focused elegance and user-focused pragmatism in the .NET ecosystem.
key_points: .NET had remarkably few self-installable blogging solutions compared to the PHP ecosystem in 2004
Critics argued .NET developers prioritized 'pretty code' over usability, installation simplicity, and whether software actually worked
Even .Text, the primary .NET option, was criticized as too complicated for average users to install and use
Atwood admits the criticism is valid based on his own experience with Community Server
The post highlights a recurring tension between developer sophistication and end-user accessibility
money_quotes: The only thing that .net developers seem to care about is pretty code/pretty language. They don't care if the software works. They don't care if the software is easily installed.
Who cares about the applications you can create with it as long as you have this wonderful language to create them with.
This leads me to my final conclusion that .net developers (especially those coding blog software) just don't 'get it.'
I'm sure in a few years after everyone is done cooing over .net itself, .net developers will start to focus back on the user instead of how nice their new toy is.
The code in WordPress is admittedly a bit messy. But any coder worth his salt can easily figure it out.
.Text is the only blogware that anyone suggested for the Windows platform.
key_insight: Developer infatuation with elegant languages and frameworks can come at the expense of building software that regular users can actually install and use.
